title: Acute Care Simulation: Patient Chart
text:
76-year-old male | MRN: 482918 | Location: ICU Bed 12


Hospital Day
1


Diet Order
NPO


Consult
SLP Swallow Evaluation


Code Status
Full Code

title: Acute Care Simulation: Patient Chart
text:
Simulation 1: Initial Patient Contact
This simulation introduces foundational skills required for acute care assessment. You will review the chart, interpret vital signs, attempt initial patient contact, and determine whether the patient is appropriate for further evaluation.

Clinical task: Determine whether Mr. Walker is appropriate for a full oral mechanism examination and clinical swallowing evaluation today.

title: Acute Care Simulation: Patient Chart
text:
Learning Objectives
Students will:

Demonstrate professional communication and rapport-building.
Gather relevant clinical information.
Perform foundational assessment procedures.
Identify factors that may influence patient care.
Apply beginning clinical reasoning skills.
Engage in self-reflection and professional growth.

title: Acute Care Simulation: Patient Chart
text:
Open the Chart


📋 Chart Review
Review history, diagnosis, orders, medications, and nursing report.
Open Chart Review


📈 Vital Signs
Review current vital signs and clinical readiness.
Open Vitals


🗣 Bedside Contact
Review patient presentation and oral mechanism exam attempt.
Open Bedside Contact


🧠 Clinical Decision
Determine recommendations and documentation plan.
Open Clinical Decision

title: Clinical Decision
text: 76-year-old male | MRN: 482918 | Location: ICU Bed 12 | Diet: NPO

title: Clinical Decision
text:
Clinical Readiness Summary
Based on chart review, vital signs, and bedside presentation, Mr. Walker is not currently appropriate for a full clinical swallowing evaluation or PO trials.
Key factors include:

Acute large left MCA stroke
Reduced level of alertness
Inconsistent command following
Poor secretion management
Inability to participate in complete oral mechanism examination
Elevated blood pressure and ongoing medical monitoring


Clinical decision: Defer PO trials and continue NPO at this time.

title: Clinical Decision
text:
Recommendations
Recommend:

Continue NPO
Frequent oral care
Medications via alternate route as medically appropriate
Continued monitoring of neurological and respiratory status
Re-attempt SLP evaluation when alertness and medical stability improve
Communicate findings with nursing and medical team

title: Clinical Decision
text:
Documentation Prompt
Use the SOAP template to document your findings.

Assessment Starter
Patient presents with suspected severe dysphagia risk in the setting of acute left MCA stroke, characterized by reduced alertness, inconsistent command following, poor secretion management, and inability to participate in formal oral mechanism or swallowing evaluation.

title: Clinical Decision
text:
Reflection Questions

What information most influenced your clinical decision-making?
What is one aspect of this case that felt clear, and one aspect that felt uncertain?
How would you explain to the medical team why PO trials were not completed today?

title: Simulation 2: Cognitive Screening
text:
92-year-old female | DOB: 09/10/1932 | Location: Acute Care / Neuro Unit


Hospital Day
1


Primary Diagnosis
Right MCA Ischemic Stroke


Consult
SLP Cognitive-Communication Evaluation


Prior Function

title: Simulation 2: Cognitive Screening
text:
Simulation 2: Cognitive Screening
This simulation focuses on chart review, clinical readiness for cognitive screening, and functional implications of cognitive-communication findings following right hemisphere stroke.

Clinical task: Determine whether Cindy is appropriate for cognitive screening today. If appropriate, administer the MoCA during simulation and use results to describe functional implications.

title: Simulation 2: Cognitive Screening
text:
Learning Focus
Students will:

Gather relevant clinical information from the medical chart.
Identify cognitive-communication risk factors after right MCA stroke.
Determine whether the patient is appropriate for cognitive screening.
Connect cognitive domains to functional skills and discharge planning.
Communicate findings using medical and patient-centered language.

title: Simulation 2: Cognitive Screening
text:
Simulation Workflow



📋 Step 1


Chart Review
Review the H&P, imaging, medications, prior level of function, and current plan of care.
Begin Chart Review




🏥 Step 2


Interdisciplinary Notes
Review nursing, OT/PT, and medical notes for functional concerns.
Review Notes




🧠 Step 3


Screening Readiness
Determine whether Cindy is appropriate for MoCA administration today.
Assess Readiness




🔎 Step 4


Functional Implications
Use cognitive domains to connect screening results to real-world function.
Review Implications

title: Screening Readiness
text: 92-year-old female | Diagnosis: Right MCA Ischemic Stroke | Assessment: MoCA if appropriate

title: Screening Readiness
text:
Clinical Readiness for Cognitive Screening
Before administering a cognitive screener, determine whether the patient can participate meaningfully.
Consider:

Alertness
Ability to attend to task
Vision/hearing needs
Motor or visual-perceptual barriers
Language abilities
Fatigue
Medical stability
Baseline function and current goals of care

title: Screening Readiness
text:
Current Presentation

Cindy is awake when you enter. She looks toward the right side of the room and does not initially notice you standing on her left side. She responds to her name and participates in brief conversation, though her orientation fluctuates. She requires occasional redirection but remains awake and engaged for short periods.

title: Screening Readiness
text:
Clinical Decision

Cindy appears appropriate for attempted cognitive screening with accommodations and clinical judgment. Monitor fatigue, provide clear instructions, and document any factors that may influence interpretation of results.

title: Screening Readiness
text:
If Proceeding With MoCA
During simulation, administer the MoCA as directed by your supervisor.
Pay attention to:

Attention and concentration
Executive functioning
Visuospatial skills
Memory
Orientation
Language
Insight and safety awareness
Impact of left neglect on task performance

title: Screening Readiness
text:
Pause and Think

What factors support proceeding with cognitive screening?
What factors may affect validity or interpretation of MoCA results?
How would you document limitations related to left neglect, fatigue, or fluctuating orientation?

title: Screening Readiness
text:
Continue
Go to Functional Implications

title: Communication Considerations
text: Assessment: Quick Aphasia Battery (QAB)

title: Communication Considerations
text:
Preparing for Assessment
Before beginning language testing, consider how communication strategies may improve participation and provide a more accurate representation of Steve’s language abilities.

title: Communication Considerations
text:
Current Presentation

Steve greets you with a smile and attempts conversation. His speech is fluent but interrupted by frequent word-finding pauses. He occasionally substitutes incorrect words and appears aware of communication breakdowns. He becomes visibly frustrated when unable to express himself.

title: Communication Considerations
text:
Clinical Considerations
Before administering the QAB:

Confirm hearing aids are available and functioning.
Reduce background noise.
Use short, clear sentences.
Present one task at a time.
Allow extra response time.
Avoid interrupting attempts to communicate.
Support communication with gestures or written keywords as appropriate.
Verify comprehension throughout the interaction.


Clinical Pearl
People with aphasia often understand more than they are able to express. Reduced verbal output should not automatically be interpreted as impaired cognition.

title: Communication Considerations
text:
Pause and Think

Which communication supports will be most important?
What barriers could affect test performance?
How will you respond if Steve becomes frustrated?

title: Communication Considerations
text:
Continue
Functional Implications

title: Interdisciplinary Notes
text: SLP Consult: Language Evaluation

title: Interdisciplinary Notes
text:
Nursing Note

Steve is pleasant and cooperative throughout the shift. Frequently pauses during conversation and appears frustrated when unable to find desired words. Follows simple directions with occasional repetition. Family reports communication appears similar to his baseline following his previous stroke.

title: Interdisciplinary Notes
text:
Physical Therapy

Patient ambulates short distances with contact guard assistance secondary to hip pain. Demonstrates good participation in therapy. Able to follow one-step commands consistently but requires repetition for more complex directions.

title: Interdisciplinary Notes
text:
Occupational Therapy

Patient independently completes basic grooming with additional time. Difficulty reading written instructions. Performs better when demonstrations accompany verbal directions.

title: Interdisciplinary Notes
text:
Physician Progress Note

History of left hemisphere ischemic stroke with residual mild aphasia. No evidence of new neurological injury following current fall. SLP consulted to reassess communication abilities during hospitalization.

title: Interdisciplinary Notes
text:
Family Report

Family reports Steve has remained largely independent since his stroke but occasionally struggles with word finding, especially when tired or stressed. They deny significant decline in cognition.

title: Interdisciplinary Notes
text:
SLP-Relevant Flags

Consider:

Expressive aphasia
Mild receptive deficits
Hearing loss
Fatigue
Emotional frustration during communication

title: Interdisciplinary Notes
text:
Pause and Think

Which findings support language assessment?
Which findings suggest cognition may be relatively preserved?
How may hearing loss influence assessment?

title: Interdisciplinary Notes
text:
Continue
Communication Considerations

title: Functional Implications
text: Focus: Language and Functional Communication

title: Functional Implications
text:
Connecting Language to Daily Function
Following completion of the Quick Aphasia Battery, consider how Steve’s communication abilities may influence daily participation and discharge planning.

title: Functional Implications
text:
Functional Communication Guide







Language Domain
Possible Functional Impact




Auditory Comprehension
Difficulty understanding medical information and multi-step directions


Verbal Expression
Difficulty communicating wants, needs, and concerns


Naming
Increased pauses and frustration during conversation


Repetition
Difficulty repeating unfamiliar medical terminology


Reading
Difficulty interpreting discharge paperwork or medication instructions


Writing
Difficulty completing written forms or communicating by writing




Aphasia affects language—not intelligence. Communication supports should maximize participation while preserving patient autonomy.

title: Functional Implications
text:
Clinical Impression Prompts
Following the QAB, consider:

Which language domains appear most impaired?
How may these deficits affect safety during hospitalization?
What communication supports should staff and family use?
What recommendations would you include in your documentation?

title: Functional Implications
text:
Possible Recommendations

Continue speech-language pathology intervention.
Educate staff regarding supported communication strategies.
Encourage use of multimodal communication.
Provide written information in simplified format.
Include family in communication strategy education.
Recommend continued outpatient or inpatient language therapy as appropriate.


Clinical Takeaway
Assessment results should extend beyond test scores to describe how language deficits affect meaningful participation in everyday communication.

Patients in acute care may experience communication barriers related to motor impairment, sensory loss, language differences, pain, or medical status.
Students will complete four website-based AAC consultation cases involving:

Quadriplegia and absent verbal communication
Visual impairment and pain communication
Spanish-language access
Hearing impairment

Students will identify communication barriers, evaluate access abilities, select appropriate AAC supports, coordinate care, and document recommendations.

title: Simulation 3: Aphasia Assessment
text:
Simulation 3: Aphasia Assessment
This simulation focuses on reviewing the medical chart, identifying communication barriers following a left hemisphere stroke, and preparing to administer a language assessment.

Clinical Task: Determine whether Steve is appropriate for language assessment, identify communication supports, and prepare to administer the Quick Aphasia Battery (QAB) during simulation.

text:
Learning Focus
Students will:

Review relevant medical information for a language evaluation.
Identify characteristics associated with aphasia.
Determine appropriate communication strategies prior to assessment.
Interpret interdisciplinary documentation.
Connect language deficits to functional communication.

title: Simulation 3: Aphasia Assessment
text:

📋 Step 1


Chart Review

Begin Chart Review




🏥 Step 2

Review Notes




💬 Step 3


Communication Considerations
Plan how to communicate effectively before beginning language assessment.
Communication Strategies




🧠 Step 4

title: Functional Implications
text: 92-year-old female | Diagnosis: Right MCA Ischemic Stroke | Focus: Cognitive domains and function

title: Functional Implications
text:
Connecting Cognitive Domains to Function
After completing the MoCA during simulation, use the results to describe how cognitive-communication deficits may affect Cindy’s daily functioning and discharge needs.

title: Functional Implications
text:
Domain-to-Function Guide







Cognitive Domain
Possible Functional Implications




Attention
Difficulty sustaining focus during therapy, meals, safety routines, and conversations


Visuospatial / Executive Function
Difficulty scanning the environment, completing multi-step tasks, navigating safely, and recognizing hazards


Memory
Difficulty recalling recent events, new safety instructions, medication changes, and therapy recommendations


Orientation
Confusion regarding place, date, situation, and hospital routines


Language
Word errors or communication breakdowns that may affect medical communication


Insight / Awareness
Reduced recognition of deficits, increased fall risk, and difficulty following safety precautions

title: Functional Implications
text:
Right Hemisphere Stroke Considerations

Right hemisphere stroke may affect attention, awareness, visual scanning, pragmatics, safety judgment, and functional problem-solving. A screening score alone does not fully describe functional impact.

title: Functional Implications
text:
Clinical Impression Prompts
Use your MoCA results and chart review to answer:

Which cognitive domains appear most impacted?
How do these deficits affect safety and participation in rehabilitation?
What recommendations would support Cindy and her spouse?
What additional assessment or follow-up may be needed?

title: Functional Implications
text:
Possible Recommendations
Recommendations may include:

Continue SLP cognitive-communication intervention
Provide family education regarding cognitive changes after right hemisphere stroke
Use environmental supports for left attention/scanning
Provide written and verbal reminders for safety information
Coordinate with OT/PT regarding neglect, mobility, and safety concerns
Consider supervision needs at discharge based on functional performance


Clinical takeaway: The goal is not only to report a score, but to explain what the findings mean for Cindy’s safety, participation, and everyday life.

title: Chart Review
text: 92-year-old female | DOB: 09/10/1932 | Diagnosis: Right MCA Ischemic Stroke

title: Chart Review
text:
Patient Snapshot



Field
Information




Name
Cindy Carlson


DOB
09/10/1932


Sex
Female


Date of Admission
10/12/2024


Symptom Onset
10/11/2024


Primary Diagnosis
Right MCA ischemic stroke


Reason for SLP Consult
Cognitive-communication evaluation


Prior Level of Function
Independent with ADLs, cognitively intact

title: Chart Review
text:
Chief Concern

Left-sided neglect, right gaze preference, fluctuating orientation, memory concerns, and difficulty with speech characterized by phonemic paraphasias.

title: Chart Review
text:
History of Present Illness

Cindy Carlson presented to the emergency department with sudden onset confusion, left-sided weakness, and difficulty speaking. Symptoms began approximately two hours prior to arrival. Initial imaging revealed an acute ischemic stroke affecting the right MCA territory.
Current clinical concerns include left-sided neglect, right gaze preference, fluctuating orientation, memory deficits for recent events, and reduced functional awareness.

title: Chart Review
text:
Imaging / Testing







Test
Findings




CT Head
Acute infarct in the right MCA distribution


MRI Brain
Ischemic stroke involving right temporal and parietal lobes


Carotid Doppler
Moderate stenosis of right internal carotid artery


Echocardiogram
No evidence of embolic source

title: Chart Review
text:
Past Medical History



Condition
Status




Type 2 diabetes mellitus
Active


Hypertension
Active


Hyperlipidemia
Active

title: Chart Review
text:
Medications



Medication
Dose / Frequency
Clinical Relevance




Aspirin
81 mg daily
Stroke prevention


Atorvastatin
40 mg daily
Hyperlipidemia / stroke prevention


Lisinopril
20 mg daily
Blood pressure management


Metformin
500 mg twice daily
Diabetes management


Clopidogrel
75 mg daily
Antiplatelet therapy

title: Chart Review
text:
Social History



Area
Information




Occupation
Retired elementary school teacher


Living Situation
Lives with spouse; married 50 years


Substance Use
Non-smoker; no alcohol use reported


Interests
Gardening, church community

title: Chart Review
text:
Medical Assessment / Plan

Right MCA ischemic stroke: Continue medical management and monitor neurological status.
Left-sided neglect: Occupational therapy to address visual-spatial deficits.
Cognitive deficits: Cognitive therapy and monitoring.
Discharge planning: Anticipate rehabilitation needs and arrange family education with spouse.

title: Chart Review
text:
Chart Review Questions

What findings suggest possible cognitive-communication impairment?
What information is most relevant to determining whether Cindy is appropriate for MoCA administration?
What functional activities may be impacted by right hemisphere stroke?
What questions would you ask nursing, OT, PT, or the spouse before completing the assessment?

title: Chart Review
text:
Continue
Go to Interdisciplinary Notes

title: Interdisciplinary Notes
text: 92-year-old female | Diagnosis: Right MCA Ischemic Stroke | Consult: Cognitive-Communication Evaluation

title: Interdisciplinary Notes
text:
Nursing Note

Time: 0715
Author: RN
Patient alert intermittently throughout morning. Oriented to name inconsistently; unable to state location or date. Frequently looks toward the right side of the room and does not attend to items placed on the left side of tray table. Requires repeated verbal cues for safety. Attempted to get out of bed without calling for assistance. Spouse at bedside and reports patient is “not acting like herself.”

title: Interdisciplinary Notes
text:
Occupational Therapy Note

OT Evaluation Summary
Patient demonstrates left-sided inattention during grooming and upper-body dressing tasks. Requires moderate cues to locate items placed on left side. Decreased awareness of left upper extremity positioning. Safety concerns noted due to reduced insight and impaired visual scanning.

title: Interdisciplinary Notes
text:
Physical Therapy Note

PT Evaluation Summary
Patient requires assistance for transfers. Demonstrates right gaze preference and reduced awareness of left side during mobility. Increased fall risk due to poor safety awareness and reduced attention to environmental obstacles.

title: Interdisciplinary Notes
text:
Physician Progress Note

Neurology Impression
Right MCA ischemic stroke with persistent left neglect, fluctuating orientation, and cognitive concerns. Continue medical management and rehabilitation therapies. SLP cognitive-communication evaluation requested to assist with discharge planning and functional recommendations.

title: Interdisciplinary Notes
text:
Family Report

Spouse reports Cindy was fully independent prior to admission, managed household routines, drove locally, gardened regularly, and participated in church activities. Spouse reports current behavior is a significant change from baseline.

title: Interdisciplinary Notes
text:
SLP-Relevant Flags

Potential concerns for SLP assessment:

Fluctuating orientation
Left-sided neglect
Reduced safety awareness
Memory deficits for recent events
Possible reduced insight into deficits
Increased fall risk
Functional change from baseline

title: Interdisciplinary Notes
text:
Pause and Think

What interdisciplinary information supports the need for cognitive-communication assessment?
What cognitive domains appear most relevant based on the notes?
How might these findings affect safety, discharge planning, and caregiver education?

title: Interdisciplinary Notes
text:
Continue
Go to Screening Readiness

title: Chart Review
text:
Consult Order

Order Type: Speech-Language Pathology Evaluation
Reason for Consult: Swallow evaluation following acute CVA
Ordering Service: Neurology
Priority: Routine
Precautions: Aspiration precautions, fall precautions

title: Chart Review
text:
History & Physical

Chief Concern: Sudden onset right-sided weakness and inability to speak.
History of Present Illness:
James Walker is a 76-year-old male admitted overnight after acute onset right-sided weakness, facial droop, and reduced verbal output. Last known well was approximately 8 hours prior to arrival. Initial neurological examination was concerning for a large left MCA stroke. Patient underwent emergent imaging and thrombectomy. He remains in the ICU for neurological monitoring.
Current Status:
Patient continues to demonstrate significant lethargy, reduced command following, and limited verbal output. Neurology requested SLP evaluation for swallowing once medically appropriate.

title: Chart Review
text:

Condition
Status




Hypertension
Active


Type II diabetes mellitus
Active


Hyperlipidemia
Active


Atrial fibrillation
Active


Former smoker
Historical

title: Chart Review
text:
Imaging / Neurology

CT Head: No acute hemorrhage. Findings concerning for evolving left MCA territory infarct.
CTA Head/Neck: Left MCA occlusion.
MRI Brain: Large acute left MCA infarct involving frontal and parietal regions.
NIH Stroke Scale: 19
Neurology Impression: Large left MCA ischemic stroke with ongoing severe neurological impairment. Continue ICU monitoring.

title: Bedside Contact
text: 76-year-old male | MRN: 482918 | Location: ICU Bed 12 | Diet: NPO

title: Bedside Contact
text:
Initial Presentation
When you enter the room, Mr. Walker is lying in bed with his eyes closed.
He requires repeated verbal stimulation to briefly open his eyes. He looks toward the clinician briefly, then closes his eyes again.

title: Bedside Contact
text:
Orientation and Command Following







Prompt
Response




“Can you tell me your name?”
No response


“Do you know where you are?”
No response


“Can you squeeze my hand?”
No response initially; one weak squeeze after repeated stimulation


“Can you open your mouth?”
No response


“Can you stick out your tongue?”
No response

title: Bedside Contact
text:
Communication

No reliable verbal response
Limited eye opening
Inconsistent command following
Reduced alertness
Wet vocal quality noted after brief vocalization attempt

title: Bedside Contact
text:
Oral Mechanism Examination Attempt

A complete oral mechanism examination cannot be completed due to reduced alertness and inability to consistently follow commands.

Observed findings:

Right facial droop
Mouth partially open at rest
Unable to protrude tongue on command
Poor secretion management
Inconsistent response to verbal cues

title: Bedside Contact
text:
Pause and Think

Is this patient appropriate for PO trials?
Can a full oral mechanism examination be completed?
What clinical information have you gathered so far?

title: AAC Consultation Resources
text: Return to Simulation 4

title: AAC Consultation Resources
text:
Acute Care AAC Toolkit
AAC includes any strategy, tool, or system used to supplement or replace speech.
In acute care, AAC is frequently used to support communication for patients experiencing:

Intubation or mechanical ventilation
Tracheostomy
Neurological injury
Motor impairment
Aphasia
Dysarthria
Hearing loss
Visual impairment
Temporary confusion
Fatigue
Language-access barriers

title: AAC Consultation Resources
text:
Low-Tech AAC Options
Low-tech AAC does not require electronic equipment.
Examples include:

Yes/no board
Alphabet board
Picture communication board
Pain scale
Body-part diagram
Written choice board
Communication notebook
Eye-gaze board
Partner-assisted scanning
Whiteboard
Gesture
Written key words

title: AAC Consultation Resources
text:
High-Tech AAC Options
High-tech AAC may include:

Tablet-based communication applications
Speech-generating devices
Eye-gaze speech-generating devices
Switch-access communication systems
Head-controlled devices
Text-to-speech applications
Personal voice amplifiers







Note



The most appropriate AAC method is the method the patient can use reliably, efficiently, and consistently. The most technologically advanced option is not always the best option.

title: AAC Consultation Resources
text:
Feature-Matching Considerations
Feature matching involves comparing the patient’s skills and needs with the characteristics of available AAC systems.







Patient Factor
AAC Considerations




Motor ability
Direct selection, eye gaze, switch access, partner-assisted scanning


Vision
Target size, contrast, spacing, lighting, field of presentation


Hearing
Written supports, amplification, visual cues


Language
Vocabulary complexity, symbol type, literacy, preferred language


Cognition
Number of choices, navigation demands, memory demands


Fatigue
Physical effort, visual effort, need for backup methods


Medical status
Positioning, respiratory support, alertness, pain


Communication needs
Pain, comfort, choices, medical decisions, family contact


Environment
Noise, staff familiarity, board placement, infection control

title: AAC Consultation Resources
text:
Establishing Yes/No Responses
A reliable yes/no response is often the foundation of functional communication.

Possible Response Modes

Verbal response
Head nod or shake
Eye movement
Blink response
Thumb movement
Finger movement
Pointing to written choices
Switch activation



Reliability Check
Ask several questions with known answers.
Include:

Questions for which the answer is yes
Questions for which the answer is no
Questions related to personal information
Questions unrelated to immediate wants or needs

Avoid relying on a single response.

title: AAC Consultation Resources
text:
Partner-Assisted Scanning
Partner-assisted scanning may be useful when the patient cannot directly point to a communication target.
The communication partner:

Presents categories or choices.
Pauses after each option.
Watches for the patient’s established response.
Confirms the selected message.
Repeats the process as needed.

Example:

Are you uncomfortable because of pain, positioning, temperature, toileting, breathing, or something else?

title: AAC Consultation Resources
text:
Common ICU Communication Categories
A bedside communication board may include:

Medical and Safety

Pain
Difficulty breathing
Suction
Nurse
Medication
Reposition
Bathroom
Call light



Comfort

Hot
Cold
Blanket
Pillow
Itching
Lights
Television
Tired



Personal Needs

Family
Phone
Glasses
Hearing aids
Food
Drink
Privacy
Stop



Emotional Needs

Scared
Frustrated
Sad
Anxious
Confused
Need information

title: AAC Consultation Resources
text:
Communication Partner Strategies
Staff and family should:

Gain the patient’s attention.
Face the patient.
Reduce distractions.
Use short, direct messages.
Ask one question at a time.
Allow adequate response time.
Verify yes/no responses.
Confirm the patient’s intended message.
Keep AAC materials within reach and view.
Document the established communication method.
Provide a backup communication strategy.

title: AAC Consultation Resources
text:
Interpreter Considerations
A qualified medical interpreter should be used for:

Medical assessment
Informed consent
Diagnosis and prognosis
Treatment education
Medical decision-making
Discharge planning
Complex symptom discussion

AAC may support immediate communication across languages but should not replace appropriate interpreter services.

title: AAC Consultation Resources
text:
Documentation Reminders
An acute care AAC note may describe:

Subjective

Reason for consultation
Patient or staff concerns
Patient-reported communication needs



Objective

Alertness
Command following
Yes/no reliability
Speech and language abilities
Reading and writing
Motor access
Vision and hearing
AAC methods trialed
Level of cueing or assistance
Patient accuracy and efficiency



Assessment

Primary communication barrier
Most effective access method
Functional implications
Need for skilled SLP services
Patient’s ability to communicate medical needs and decisions



Plan

Recommended AAC system
Staff and family education
Bedside signage
Interdisciplinary coordination
Follow-up
Need for additional assessment

title: AAC Consultation Resources
text:
Example Assessment Statement

Pt presents with impaired functional communication secondary to absent verbal output and severe motor limitations. Pt demonstrated reliable responses to concrete yes/no questions using upward eye gaze for yes and downward eye gaze for no. Pt successfully selected basic medical and comfort needs from a low-tech eye-gaze communication board with minimal cueing. Recommend continued use of established eye-gaze system with partner-assisted scanning available as a backup method.

title: AAC Consultation Resources
text:
Example Plan Statement

Continue skilled ST intervention to establish an efficient and reliable AAC system for communication of basic wants, medical needs, and care preferences. Provide ongoing staff and family education and coordinate with OT and nursing regarding positioning, access, and bedside implementation.

title: AAC Consultation Resources
text:
End-of-Simulation Reflection
After completing all four cases, consider:

How did motor, sensory, cognitive, linguistic, and environmental factors affect AAC selection?
When was low-tech AAC more appropriate than high-tech AAC?
How did the immediate medical context influence your recommendations?
What information would you communicate to nursing at the end of each consultation?
What aspects of AAC assessment and implementation require additional practice?

title: Case A: Quadriplegia and Loss of Speech
text: 38-year-old male | Consult: AAC Evaluation

title: Case A: Quadriplegia and Loss of Speech
text:
Patient Snapshot



Patient Information
Details




Name
Benjamin Carter


Age
38


Admitting Diagnosis
C3–C4 spinal cord injury


Current Setting
Intensive Care Unit


Respiratory Status
Mechanically ventilated


Communication Status
Unable to produce functional speech


Motor Status
Quadriplegia with minimal voluntary head movement


Cognitive Status
Alert and able to follow commands


Language
English


Family
Partner visits regularly

title: Case A: Quadriplegia and Loss of Speech
text:
Chief Concern

The patient has frequently attempted to gain the attention of staff. Nursing reports that the patient appears frustrated and uncomfortable.
Through informal yes/no questioning, staff believe that the patient may be experiencing:

A headache
Discomfort related to positioning
A recent episode of urinary incontinence
A desire to speak with their partner

Nursing has not yet established a consistent communication system.


Immediate Clinical Concern: The patient currently lacks a reliable method for communicating pain, physical discomfort, hygiene needs, and requests for assistance.

title: Case A: Quadriplegia and Loss of Speech
text:
Step 1: Identify the Communication Barrier
Consider the following:

Why is verbal communication unavailable?
What communication abilities appear to be preserved?
What information is still needed before selecting an AAC method?
Are the patient’s responses reliable and consistent?
Does the patient have sufficient vision for an eye-gaze system?
Can the patient move their eyes vertically and horizontally?
Is head movement reliable enough to use as an access method?
Can the patient tolerate sustained visual attention?

title: Case A: Quadriplegia and Loss of Speech
section: Step 2: Establish a Reliable Yes/No Response
text:
Step 2: Establish a Reliable Yes/No Response
Before introducing a larger communication system, determine whether the patient has a reliable yes/no response.
Possible response modes include:

Looking upward for yes and downward for no
Looking toward a written YES or NO
Blinking once for yes and twice for no
Moving the head in a consistent direction
Using partner-assisted scanning

Test the response using questions with known answers.
Examples:

Is your name Benjamin?
Are you currently in the hospital?
Are you 10 years old?
Is your partner’s name Maria?


A reliable yes/no response should be verified across multiple questions before it is used for medical decision-making.

title: Case A: Quadriplegia and Loss of Speech
text:
Step 3: Evaluate Access Skills
Determine which access methods may be feasible.

Motor Access

Can the patient move either hand?
Can the patient point?
Can the patient move their head?
Can the patient activate a switch?
Are eye movements consistent?
Does fatigue affect performance?



Sensory Access

Can the patient see large written words?
Can the patient visually scan multiple options?
Does the patient require glasses?
Can the patient hear verbal choices?
Is background noise affecting performance?



Cognitive-Linguistic Skills

Can the patient follow one-step directions?
Can the patient read single words?
Can the patient identify pictures?
Can the patient make selections from a field of choices?
Can the patient maintain attention long enough to use a communication system?

title: Case A: Quadriplegia and Loss of Speech
text:
Step 4: Select AAC Supports
Which supports would you trial?

Eye-gaze communication board
Large-print yes/no board
Partner-assisted auditory scanning
Pain-rating board
Body-part or positioning board
Alphabet board
Speech-generating device with eye-gaze access
Single-message switch
Call system adapted for head or eye movement

Consider:

Which option offers the fastest access to urgent needs?
Which option requires the least physical effort?
Which option can nursing staff use consistently across shifts?
What backup method should be available if the primary system fails?

title: Case A: Quadriplegia and Loss of Speech
text:
Step 5: Address Immediate Needs
Using the patient’s established response method, determine the priority of the following concerns:

Headache
Repositioning
Incontinence and hygiene
Contacting the patient’s partner

Consider how you would communicate each need to the appropriate team member.







Patient Need
Appropriate Follow-Up




Headache
Notify nursing and support pain assessment


Positioning discomfort
Notify nursing and/or physical or occupational therapy


Hygiene needs
Notify nursing staff


Contact partner
Confirm patient consent and assist with communication

title: Case A: Quadriplegia and Loss of Speech
text:
Step 6: Interdisciplinary Coordination
Which team members may need to participate?

Nursing
Respiratory therapy
Occupational therapy
Physical therapy
Physician or advanced practice provider
Family or communication partner
Assistive technology specialist

Consider what education should be placed at the bedside.
Examples:

The patient’s established yes/no response
Positioning instructions for the communication board
Instructions for partner-assisted scanning
A list of frequently requested messages
The patient’s preferred communication method
Instructions for verifying responses

title: Case A: Quadriplegia and Loss of Speech
text:
Documentation Task
Complete a SOAP note that includes:

The reason for consultation
Communication and access skills assessed
Reliability of yes/no responses
AAC strategies trialed
Level of assistance required
Immediate needs identified
Patient and staff education
Interdisciplinary recommendations
Plan for follow-up

title: Case A: Quadriplegia and Loss of Speech
text:
Reflection Questions

How did you determine the order in which the patient’s immediate needs should be addressed?
Which AAC method was most appropriate for this patient, and what patient-specific factors supported your choice?
What backup communication method would you recommend if fatigue or visual changes reduced the patient’s eye-gaze accuracy?



Reveal Example Clinical Reasoning

The patient is alert, follows commands, and has severe motor limitations with no functional speech. Eye movement may represent the patient’s most reliable access method.
An initial low-tech system could include a large-print yes/no board and an eye-gaze communication board containing urgent messages such as pain, positioning, suction, bathroom, family, hot, cold, and call nurse.
Partner-assisted scanning could serve as a backup method. The communication partner would verbally present categories and choices while the patient uses the established yes/no response.
A high-tech eye-gaze speech-generating device could be considered if the communication impairment is expected to continue and the patient demonstrates adequate visual attention and language skills.
The most urgent concerns are pain, positioning, and hygiene. Nursing should be notified immediately, while the patient’s request to contact their partner should be addressed after consent is confirmed.

title: Case A: Quadriplegia and Loss of Speech
text:
Continue
Go to Case B

title: Case B: Visual Impairment and Pain
text: 71-year-old female | Consult: AAC Evaluation

title: Case B: Visual Impairment and Pain
text:
Patient Snapshot







Patient Information
Details




Name
Patricia Lewis


Age
71


Admitting Diagnosis
Left hip fracture following a fall


Current Setting
Acute Care Orthopedic Unit


Communication Status
Verbal but inconsistently communicates needs


Vision
Corrective lenses left at home


Hearing
Functional for conversational speech


Motor Status
Able to move upper extremities; movement limited by pain


Cognitive Status
Mild confusion noted following pain medication


Language
English


Family
Daughter listed as primary contact

title: Case B: Visual Impairment and Pain
text:
Chief Concern

The patient reports sharp left hip pain rated as 6/10. She repeatedly asks when she may receive her next pain medication.
She also reports:

Difficulty getting comfortable in bed
Needing an additional pillow
Difficulty reading the room whiteboard
Wanting her daughter to bring her glasses
Uncertainty regarding the date and time

Staff have attempted to provide written information, but the patient reports that the print is too small to read.


Immediate Clinical Concern: A visual communication tool will not be effective unless its size, contrast, spacing, and presentation are adapted to the patient’s visual needs.

title: Case B: Visual Impairment and Pain
text:
Step 1: Identify the Communication Barrier
Consider:

Is the primary barrier speech, language, cognition, vision, pain, or a combination?
How may pain and medication affect attention and memory?
Can the patient reliably understand spoken information?
Can the patient read large print?
Can the patient point to large visual targets?
Would a verbal or auditory system be more effective than a picture board?

title: Case B: Visual Impairment and Pain
section: Step 2: Brief Communication and Cognitive Screening
text:
Step 2: Brief Communication and Cognitive Screening
Determine whether the patient can:

State her name
Identify that she is in the hospital
State the reason for hospitalization
Follow one-step directions
Answer simple yes/no questions
Repeat back information regarding pain medication
Recall information following a brief delay
Identify large-print words or symbols
Point to a target using either hand

Consider whether inaccurate responses reflect:

Reduced hearing
Reduced vision
Pain
Medication effects
Delirium
Language impairment
Baseline cognitive impairment

title: Case B: Visual Impairment and Pain
text:
Step 3: Evaluate Visual Access
Trial adjustments such as:

Large-print written choices
High-contrast text
Reduced number of choices
Increased spacing between targets
Verbal presentation of choices
A color-coded pain scale
Presentation within the patient’s intact visual field
Improved lighting
Reduced visual clutter


Potential Trial
Present a pain board with:

Large numbers
A simple 0–10 scale
Written descriptors
High visual contrast
Adequate spacing between selections

Then confirm the patient’s selection verbally.

title: Case B: Visual Impairment and Pain
section: Step 4: Select AAC and Communication Supports
text:
Step 4: Select AAC and Communication Supports
Which supports would you trial?

Large-print pain scale
High-contrast picture board
Large-print written choice board
Auditory choice presentation
Simple yes/no questions
Whiteboard with enlarged information
Orientation board
Bedside schedule
Tablet with enlarged icons
Verbal rehearsal or teach-back
Communication partner support

Consider:

Is the patient’s speech functional?
Does she primarily need expressive AAC or environmental communication support?
How will you ensure that she understands the medication schedule?
What should staff do if her confusion increases?

title: Case B: Visual Impairment and Pain
text:
Step 5: Address Immediate Needs
The patient communicates the following:

Pain is currently 6/10.
She would like medication.
She wants another pillow.
She would like her daughter to bring her glasses.
She does not remember when medication was last administered.

Determine the appropriate response.







Patient Need
Appropriate Follow-Up




Pain medication
Notify nursing; do not independently promise medication


Medication schedule
Provide simplified information in coordination with nursing


Pillow and positioning
Notify nursing staff


Glasses
Obtain consent and contact family


Orientation support
Update large-print bedside information




The SLP may support the patient’s ability to understand and communicate about pain. Medication decisions remain within the responsibility of nursing and the medical team.

title: Case B: Visual Impairment and Pain
text:
Step 6: Patient and Staff Education
Education may include:

Use large print and high contrast.
Present one idea or question at a time.
Reduce the number of visual choices.
Confirm the patient’s message verbally.
Use teach-back for important medical information.
Keep the communication board within reach.
Reassess communication when pain or alertness changes.
Ensure the patient’s glasses are available as soon as possible.

title: Case B: Visual Impairment and Pain
text:
Documentation Task
Complete a SOAP note that includes:

Communication barriers identified
Cognitive, motor, and sensory abilities observed
Visual accommodations trialed
Pain communication supports used
Patient response to AAC strategies
Education provided
Family and nursing coordination
Recommendations and follow-up plan

title: Case B: Visual Impairment and Pain
text:
Reflection Questions

How did you determine whether the patient’s difficulty was primarily related to vision, cognition, pain, or language?
Which communication tools were most effective given the patient’s visual limitations?
How would you ensure that the patient accurately understood when she could receive her next medication dose?



Reveal Example Clinical Reasoning

The patient retains functional verbal communication but has difficulty accessing standard written information because her corrective lenses are unavailable. Pain, medication, and mild confusion may also affect her ability to retain information.
A large-print, high-contrast pain scale and enlarged written choices may support communication. Auditory presentation and teach-back should be used for important information, including the pain medication schedule.
The SLP should not independently interpret or promise medication timing. Nursing should provide the confirmed schedule, and the patient should be asked to repeat the information in her own words.
With the patient’s permission, the daughter may be contacted and asked to bring the patient’s glasses. Staff should continue to monitor whether confusion changes after pain management or over the course of hospitalization.

title: Case B: Visual Impairment and Pain
text:
Continue
Go to Case C

title: Case D: Hearing Impairment
text: 79-year-old male | Consult: AAC Evaluation

title: Case D: Hearing Impairment
text:
Patient Snapshot



Patient Information
Details




Name
Robert Greene


Age
79


Admitting Diagnosis
Right shoulder fracture following a fall


Current Setting
Acute Care Medical-Surgical Unit


Communication Status
Verbal


Hearing
Bilateral hearing impairment; hearing aids at home


Vision
Functional with glasses


Motor Status
Right arm immobilized; left hand functional


Cognitive Status
Alert; staff report inconsistent responses


Language
English


Family
Spouse available by telephone

title: Case D: Hearing Impairment
text:
Chief Concern

The patient has difficulty hearing staff, particularly when staff speak from outside his visual field or while wearing a mask.
The patient has attempted to communicate the following:

Right shoulder pain rated as 3/10
Need for restroom assistance
Difficulty using the television controls
Desire to activate closed captioning
Request for his spouse to bring his hearing aids

Staff are uncertain whether some incorrect responses reflect confusion or reduced auditory access.


Immediate Clinical Concern: A patient may appear confused, uncooperative, or cognitively impaired when spoken information is not accessible.

title: Case D: Hearing Impairment
text:
Step 1: Identify the Communication Barrier
Consider:

Can the patient understand written language?
Can the patient read lips or facial cues?
Does masking affect communication?
Does the patient hear better in one ear?
Is the room noisy?
Can the patient use a personal amplifier?
Can the patient write with the unaffected hand?
Are inaccurate responses reduced when information is written?

title: Case D: Hearing Impairment
text:
Step 2: Modify the Environment
Before assuming cognitive impairment, trial:

Gain the patient’s attention before speaking.
Face the patient directly.
Reduce background noise.
Ensure adequate lighting.
Speak clearly at a normal rate.
Avoid shouting.
Present one message at a time.
Use written key words.
Use a whiteboard.
Provide a personal amplifier when available.
Confirm understanding through teach-back.

title: Case D: Hearing Impairment
text:
Step 3: Evaluate Communication Modalities
Determine whether the patient can:

Understand large-print written questions
Read a whiteboard
Point to yes/no choices
Write short responses with the left hand
Follow directions when visual cues are provided
Use gestures
Understand speech with amplification
Use bedside call and television controls

Consider comparing performance across conditions:

Spoken instruction only
Spoken instruction while facing the patient
Spoken instruction plus written key words
Written instruction only
Spoken instruction with amplification

title: Case D: Hearing Impairment
text:
Step 4: Select Communication Supports
Which supports would you trial?

Whiteboard
Large-print written choices
Yes/no board
Personal voice amplifier
Written pain scale
Gesture
Closed captioning
Bedside communication card
Communication partner strategies
Tablet-based text
Family-provided hearing aids

Consider:

Which method is fastest for immediate needs?
Which method can staff use consistently?
What should be documented at the bedside?
How will you verify that the hearing aids are functioning when they arrive?

title: Case D: Hearing Impairment
text:
Step 5: Address Immediate Needs
The patient communicates:

Shoulder pain is 3/10.
He needs restroom assistance.
He wants closed captions activated.
He would like his spouse to bring his hearing aids.

Determine the appropriate response.



Patient Need
Appropriate Follow-Up




Shoulder pain
Notify nursing and support pain communication


Restroom assistance
Notify nursing immediately


Television captions
Assist directly or coordinate with staff


Hearing aids
Confirm consent and contact spouse


Communication access
Establish bedside communication strategies

title: Case D: Hearing Impairment
text:
Step 6: Patient and Staff Education
Education may include:

Approach from the front.
Gain attention before speaking.
Reduce environmental noise.
Face the patient during communication.
Use written key words and short sentences.
Confirm understanding.
Keep the whiteboard within reach.
Use closed captions.
Avoid interpreting reduced auditory access as cognitive impairment.
Ensure hearing aids are labeled and stored safely when not in use.

title: Case D: Hearing Impairment
text:
Documentation Task
Complete a SOAP note that includes:

Hearing and communication barriers identified
Environmental modifications trialed
Communication modalities assessed
Patient performance with written versus spoken information
AAC or amplification supports provided
Immediate needs identified
Staff and family education
Recommendations and follow-up plan

title: Case D: Hearing Impairment
text:
Reflection Questions

Which communication methods were most effective for this patient?
How did you distinguish reduced hearing access from possible cognitive impairment?
What strategies should nursing staff use consistently across shifts?



Reveal Example Clinical Reasoning

The patient’s verbal expression is functional, but access to spoken information is limited because his hearing aids are unavailable. Written communication, direct visual access to the speaker, environmental modifications, and amplification should be trialed before drawing conclusions about cognition.
A whiteboard or large-print written choice board may support immediate communication. A personal amplifier may improve access until the patient’s hearing aids arrive.
Staff should approach from the front, reduce background noise, face the patient, use written key words, and confirm comprehension. Closed captioning should be activated to improve environmental access.
With the patient’s consent, his spouse may be contacted and asked to bring the hearing aids. Nursing should address pain and restroom needs.

title: Case D: Hearing Impairment
text:
Continue
Review AAC Resources

title: Case C: Spanish-Speaking Patient
text: 63-year-old female | Consult: AAC Evaluation

title: Case C: Spanish-Speaking Patient
text:
Patient Snapshot







Patient Information
Details




Name
Rosa Martínez


Age
63


Admitting Diagnosis
Community-acquired pneumonia


Current Setting
Acute Care Medical Unit


Communication Status
Verbal in Spanish


English Proficiency
Minimal


Motor Status
Able to point and gesture


Vision
Functional


Hearing
No known impairment


Cognitive Status
Alert; formal cognitive status not yet established


Preferred Language
Spanish


Family
Adult son listed as emergency contact

title: Case C: Spanish-Speaking Patient
text:
Chief Concern

The patient has attempted to communicate several needs to staff. Staff believe she may be indicating:

Hunger
Feeling cold
Itching
A desire to contact her family

The patient appears frustrated when staff do not understand her. A family member has occasionally interpreted by telephone, but a qualified medical interpreter has not consistently been available during routine care.


Language Difference Is Not a Communication Disorder: Limited English proficiency should not be interpreted as aphasia, cognitive impairment, confusion, or reduced decision-making capacity.

title: Case C: Spanish-Speaking Patient
text:
Step 1: Identify the Communication Barrier
Consider:

Does the patient have a communication impairment, a language-access barrier, or both?
Is a qualified medical interpreter required?
What information can be supported temporarily through pictures or gestures?
Which conversations should not rely solely on a picture board?
Is the patient able to read in Spanish?
Are cultural and dialect differences relevant?
Has the patient’s preferred language been documented?

title: Case C: Spanish-Speaking Patient
text:
Step 2: Establish Immediate Functional Communication
Use appropriate temporary supports to determine whether the patient is:

Hungry
Thirsty
Cold
Hot
Itching
In pain
Short of breath
In need of toileting assistance
Requesting family
Requesting nursing assistance

Possible supports include:

Picture symbols
Spanish-language written choices
Gestures
Demonstration
Yes/no questions interpreted by a qualified interpreter
A bilingual basic-needs board
Pointing to body parts
A pain scale adapted to the patient’s language and literacy level

title: Case C: Spanish-Speaking Patient
text:
Step 3: Consider Interpreter Use
A qualified medical interpreter should be used for:

Medical history
Informed consent
Discussion of diagnosis or prognosis
Complex symptom descriptions
Treatment education
Decision-making
Discharge planning
Formal assessment
Conversations involving risk or safety

A family member should not routinely replace a qualified interpreter for medical communication.

AAC may supplement language access, but it does not replace a qualified medical interpreter.

title: Case C: Spanish-Speaking Patient
text:
Step 4: Evaluate AAC Access
Determine whether the patient can:

Point accurately to pictures
Match a picture to a spoken Spanish word
Read basic Spanish words
Answer yes/no questions in Spanish
Use gestures consistently
Navigate a small field of choices
Use a telephone or tablet
Communicate preferences with an interpreter present

Consider whether the board should include:

Pictures only
Spanish words
Spanish and English words
A pain scale
Body-part images
Family and telephone icons
Food and drink choices
Temperature and comfort needs

title: Case C: Spanish-Speaking Patient
text:
Step 5: Address Immediate Needs
The patient appears to communicate that:

She is cold.
She is hungry.
Her arm is itching.
She wants to call her son.

Determine the appropriate follow-up.



Patient Need
Appropriate Follow-Up




Cold
Notify nursing and assist with comfort measures


Hunger
Confirm diet status with nursing before offering food


Itching
Notify nursing for assessment


Call family
Confirm consent and assist with contact


Medical questions
Obtain a qualified interpreter




Immediate Clinical Concern: Do not provide food or drink until the patient’s diet status and swallowing recommendations have been confirmed.

title: Case C: Spanish-Speaking Patient
text:
Step 6: Interdisciplinary Coordination
Potential team members include:

Qualified medical interpreter
Nursing
Physician or advanced practice provider
Dietary services
Respiratory therapy
Family
Case management
Speech-language pathology

Staff education may include:

Document the patient’s preferred language.
Use a qualified interpreter for medical discussions.
Keep the bilingual basic-needs board within reach.
Speak directly to the patient, not to the interpreter.
Use short, clear messages.
Allow additional response time.
Confirm meaning rather than assuming.
Avoid treating language difference as cognitive impairment.

title: Case C: Spanish-Speaking Patient
text:
Documentation Task
Complete a SOAP note that includes:

The patient’s preferred language
Communication barriers observed
Interpreter use
Functional communication abilities
AAC supports trialed
Patient response
Immediate needs communicated
Education provided to staff
Recommendations for language access and follow-up

title: Case C: Spanish-Speaking Patient
text:
Reflection Questions

How did you distinguish a language-access barrier from a possible communication disorder?
Which AAC resources were most effective for supporting immediate basic needs?
Which aspects of this patient’s care require a qualified medical interpreter rather than a communication board or family member?



Reveal Example Clinical Reasoning

The patient is verbally communicative in Spanish. The immediate barrier is limited shared language between the patient and staff rather than confirmed speech, language, or cognitive impairment.
A bilingual picture-and-word board may help the patient communicate urgent basic needs such as pain, temperature, hunger, toileting, itching, and family contact. However, this board should supplement rather than replace a qualified medical interpreter.
A qualified interpreter is necessary for formal assessment, medical education, informed consent, complex symptom discussion, and care planning.
The patient’s request for food should be communicated to nursing, and diet status should be confirmed before food or drink is provided. Nursing should also address itching and comfort needs. With consent, staff may help the patient contact her son.

title: Case C: Spanish-Speaking Patient
text:
Continue
Go to Case D

title: Simulation 4: AAC Consultation
text: Acute Care Simulation | Consultation-Based Activity

title: Simulation 4: AAC Consultation
text:
Simulation Overview
Patients in acute care may experience temporary or permanent barriers to communication related to neurological injury, intubation, motor impairment, sensory loss, language differences, pain, fatigue, or changes in cognition.
In this simulation, you will assume the role of the consulting speech-language pathologist. You will review several patient scenarios, identify barriers to functional communication, and recommend appropriate augmentative and alternative communication (AAC) supports.
Unlike previous simulations, this activity is primarily completed through the course website rather than through a mannequin-based encounter.

Clinical Focus
The goal of an acute care AAC consultation is not necessarily to identify the most advanced communication device. The immediate goal is to establish a reliable and efficient way for the patient to communicate medical needs, preferences, questions, and decisions.

title: Simulation 4: AAC Consultation
text:
Learning Objectives
By the end of this simulation, students will be able to:

Identify barriers affecting functional communication in the acute care setting.
Evaluate patient access abilities, including motor, visual, auditory, cognitive, and linguistic factors.
Select appropriate low-tech and high-tech AAC supports.
Develop strategies for communicating urgent medical and personal needs.
Provide patient, family, and staff education regarding AAC use.
Coordinate recommendations with the interdisciplinary team.
Document findings and recommendations using an acute care SOAP note.
Reflect on clinical reasoning and patient-centered decision-making.

title: Simulation 4: AAC Consultation
text:
Your Role
You are the speech-language pathologist responding to a communication consultation.
For each patient case, you will:

Review the reason for consultation.
Identify the primary communication barrier.
Evaluate available communication and access skills.
Select AAC strategies to trial.
Address the patient’s immediate communication needs.
Identify appropriate interdisciplinary follow-up.
Document your assessment and recommendations.

title: Simulation 4: AAC Consultation
text:
AAC Assessment Framework
When selecting an AAC approach, consider the following areas:







Area
Questions to Consider




Communication
Can the patient speak, vocalize, gesture, write, read, or answer yes/no questions?


Language
Does the patient understand spoken and written language? What is the patient’s preferred language?


Cognition
Can the patient attend, remember directions, make selections, and understand cause and effect?


Motor Access
Can the patient point, reach, lift a hand, move their head, or use eye gaze?


Vision
Can the patient see written words, pictures, colors, and communication targets?


Hearing
Can the patient hear conversational speech? Are hearing aids or amplification available?


Environment
Are noise, lighting, positioning, fatigue, or medical equipment affecting communication?


Urgency
What medical, safety, comfort, or personal needs must be communicated immediately?

title: Simulation 4: AAC Consultation
text:
Available Patient Consultations

Student Task: Complete each patient case and record your findings in the SOAP note provided by your instructor.


Case A: Quadriplegia and Loss of Speech
A patient with quadriplegia is unable to verbally communicate basic needs and has limited voluntary movement.
Begin Case A



Case B: Visual Impairment and Pain
A patient does not have access to their glasses and is experiencing pain, discomfort, and difficulty communicating immediate needs.
Begin Case B



Case C: Spanish-Speaking Patient
A patient who primarily communicates in Spanish needs assistance expressing basic needs and contacting family.
Begin Case C



Case D: Hearing Impairment
A patient does not have access to their hearing aids and is having difficulty communicating with staff.
Begin Case D

title: Simulation 4: AAC Consultation
text:
Additional Resources
Review AAC tools, clinical considerations, and documentation reminders before or during the activity.
View AAC Resources
